column1: How are digital signals represented in terms of time and amplitude?
column2: Digital signals are discrete-time and discrete-amplitude.

column1: Explain why digital signals are highly immune to noise compared to analog signals.
column2: Digital signals can be perfectly regenerated using repeaters because distorted pulses are still recognisable as finite logic levels.

column1: Under what conditions is power mostly drawn in digital CMOS technology?
column2: Power is primarily drawn only during state transitions.

column1: Contrast the storage of analog data with digital data retrieval.
column2: Analog storage is prone to degradation over time, whereas digital binary bits allow for perfect retrieval with zero degradation.

column1: Why is digital data considered more secure than analog signals?
column2: Digital data can be easily encrypted using algorithms like AES or RSA before transmission.

column1: In decimal-to-binary integer conversion, which remainder represents the Most Significant Bit (MSB)?
column2: The last remainder produced by the division process.

column1: Which method is used to convert a number in base-$r$ back to a decimal number?
column2: Polynomial expansion weighting.

column1: Describe the function of a NOT gate.
column2: The output is the logical reversal of the input.

column1: Define 'Universal Gates'.
column2: Gates that can be used to construct any other logic gate, such as NAND and NOR.

column1: Which gate acts as a 'Difference Detector', outputting $1$ only when inputs are different?
column2: XOR (Exclusive OR) gate.

column1: Which gate acts as an 'Equality Detector', outputting $1$ only when inputs are identical?
column2: XNOR (Exclusive NOR) gate.

column1: An XOR gate is said to have _____ Parity logic.
column2: Odd

column1: An XNOR gate is said to have _____ Parity logic.
column2: Even

column1: Describe the function of a Buffer gate.
column2: The output is logically identical to the input.

column1: State one practical reason for using a Buffer gate in a circuit.
column2: Signal amplification to drive more inputs (Fan-out) or intentional propagation delay.

column1: Inverting the output of an XNOR gate results in which logic function?
column2: XOR

column1: State the Principle of Duality in Boolean Algebra.
column2: A valid equation remains valid if AND/OR operators and $0/1$ constants are swapped.

column1: Boolean Algebra: $A \cdot 1 =$
column2: A (Identity Law)

column1: Boolean Algebra: $A \cdot A' =$
column2: 0 (Inverse Law)

column1: State the dual form of the Distributive Law ($A \cdot (B + C) = A \cdot B + A \cdot C$).
column2: $A + (B \cdot C) = (A + B) \cdot (A + C)$

column1: Boolean Algebra: $A + A \cdot B =$
column2: A (Absorption Law)

column1: Why do engineers use complements to perform subtraction in digital systems?
column2: It allows the hardware to perform subtraction using only addition circuits, saving space and complexity.

column1: What are the two types of complements available for any base $r$?
column2: Radix complement ($r$'s) and Diminished Radix complement ($r-1$'s).

column1: How is the 1's complement of a binary number calculated?
column2: Every bit is flipped (0 becomes 1, 1 becomes 0).

column1: Under what condition is the Sum bit of a Full Adder HIGH ($1$)?
column2: If the number of input 1s is ODD.

column1: Under what condition is the Carry bit of a Full Adder HIGH ($1$)?
column2: If there are two or more 1s in the inputs (Majority Gate logic).

column1: Identify the logic gate that corresponds to the 'Sum' output of a binary adder.
column2: XOR gate.

column1: Define 'Fan-out' in the context of digital logic.
column2: The number of gate inputs that a single output can reliably drive.

column1: What is the hexadecimal value of the binary string $1010$?
In binary integer conversion, which bit is the Least Significant Bit (LSB)?
column2: The first remainder produced by the division process.

column1: Boolean Algebra: $A + A' \cdot B =$
column2: $A + B$ (using the Distributive Dual Law).
